policy_statement: Electrify 30% of total existing residential buildings by 2035.
mechanism_description: Electrifying existing homes replaces on-site fossil fuel end uses with electric equipment, reducing direct building combustion emissions (and further lowering total emissions as the grid decarbonizes).
primary_causal_pathway: Fossil fuel end uses in homes → electrified end uses (heat pumps/induction/etc.) → ↓ on-site combustion + (often) ↓ energy use → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human/system activity: Retrofit existing residential buildings to use electric equipment instead of gas/oil/propane appliances (e.g., heat pumps for space and water heating, induction stoves, heat-pump dryers), sometimes paired with electrical panel/service upgrades.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced or eliminated combustion of fossil fuels at the building, and in many cases higher end-use efficiency (notably heat pumps moving heat rather than generating it). Remaining emissions shift upstream to power generation.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂ (and to a lesser extent CH₄/N₂O) emissions from building operations; total emissions reductions strengthen over time as the electricity grid becomes lower-carbon.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from eliminating (or greatly reducing) on-site fossil fuel combustion in residential buildings, with additional reductions as the power grid decarbonizes.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Operational fossil fuel displacement in homes leads to lower GHG emissions (directly and via cleaner electricity over time).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While electrification can also improve energy efficiency or indoor air quality, the defining and largest climate effect across all instances is emissions mitigation via reduced combustion and reduced carbon intensity of delivered energy.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—resource-efficiency gains (lower energy input) are common but not guaranteed in every electrification instance (e.g., resistance heating), and adaptation benefits are context-dependent rather than inherent.  
4) Edge cases applied: If electrification is served by a very carbon-intensive grid, near-term GHG benefits can be smaller, but the mechanism is still fundamentally aimed at emissions reduction through fuel switching and typically anticipates grid decarbonization.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy implementing “residential building electrification” is principally a mitigation strategy because it targets operational emissions from building energy use.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Near-term net GHG impact can vary with grid emissions intensity and technology choice (heat pump vs. resistance), and some programs bundle efficiency measures; however, the mechanism’s core intent and typical outcome remain emissions mitigation via fuel switching.
co_benefits: Potential indoor air quality improvements; potential household energy cost savings; potential public health benefits from reduced combustion pollutants
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument type ambiguity: without accompanying policy levers (rebates/mandates/standards), the text is purely a target; implementation could later involve enabling instruments, but they are not specified here.
